title: One Small Step Can Change Your Life
author: Robert Maurer

chapters.title: Change is not as difficult as it seems
chapters.text:
Change is constant. Everyone knows that. But change itself is not easy because it means adopting newer ways of thinking and doing things. However, many people aren't ready for that.

Here's a common proof we can all identify with: everyone sets New Year's resolutions. But how many people stick to them?

Not many.

According to Robert Maurer, on average, an American repeats the same New Year's resolutions for a decade without any alteration; those who do succeed often fail five or six attempts prior.

Set your New Year's resolutions with the “how” question.

The same thing applies to organizations. At the start of each year, companies set goals for different departments, and many end up underperforming. But the kaizen way can change this on both personal and corporate levels.

When you improve a little each day, eventually big things occur. ~ John Wooden

Kaizen is all about the process of using small steps for continual improvement. It describes the ability to use minimal effort to improve a habit, a process, or a product. The first known application of this philosophy was during the Great Depression in America (though not known as “kaizen” then).

Kaizen philosophy was introduced in Japan after the Second World War when General Douglas MacArthur's occupation forces rebuilt the destroyed country, taking it one step at a time.

Kaizen is an effective and easy method for achieving a specific objective. The individual adoption of kaizen consists of six diverse tactics, which encompass:

• Using small questions to dispel fear.
• Developing new skills and habits by thinking small thoughts.
• Taking small actions toward desired goals.
• Solving minor problems amid a crisis.
• Encouraging yourself by celebrating milestones on your way to desired outcomes.
• Staying mindful so you can experience crucial moments others don't see.

In contrast to common beliefs, change doesn't necessitate suffering and should not be a tool to coerce someone into significant action. This summary guides you in effectively removing obstacles that prevent individuals and teams from realizing their goals. It instructs you to use your brain's structure to foster change easily.

chapters.title: The Kaizen Technique deactivates fear
chapters.text:
Change can be scary, which is why many attempts to reach goals through revolutionary means often fail. Nevertheless, it is possible to use the idea of kaizen to disarm the brain's fear response and awaken the genius in all of us.

The fear of change is rooted in the human brain.

The human brain has three parts: the stem, the midbrain, and the cortex. The cortex, which started developing 102,000,000 years ago, is responsible for the miracle of being human. You need access to the cortex to create lasting change and enhance civilization. It is also worth noting that this access is often restricted — the brain has developed so that any new challenge, opportunity, or desire triggers a degree of fear.

Some people overcome it by converting fear into positive emotions, such as excitement. The greater the obstacle, the more invigorated, effective, and exhilarated they are.

The kaizen approach of taking small steps can also solve this challenge. You can use tiny, easily achievable goals to “go past” the amygdala and avoid setting off alarm bells (the amygdala is the part of the brain that alerts the body to prepare for action). By consistently practicing incremental steps, your cortex becomes active, aiding you in realizing the intended change thus facilitating the formation of new habits. In time, consistent small steps weaken your resistance to change.

Remember never to let fear and roadblocks to change make you feel so frustrated that you stop trying to improve. Fear is a gift from the body because it alerts us to what's ahead. Use this emotion to your advantage rather than letting it hinder your progress.

Did you know? Unlike other organs that have remained fairly consistent through human evolution, the brain has continued to change.

chapters.title: Asking small questions elevates your creativity
chapters.text:
In Japanese business, the kaizen philosophy motivates employees to remain alert for the company's benefit, resulting in enhanced profitability and decreased expenses.

Using kaizen in this manner requires making simple requests restricted in scope. Simplicity gets a lot done!

This approach improves both the quality and quantity of employee responses. Employees derive fulfillment from enacting their ideas and enhancing their daily tasks. Additionally, they experience the joy of being proactive and involved in their work.

Don't look for the quick, big improvement. Seek the small improvement one day at a time. ~ John Wooden

The brain, which doubles in size after the first year of our lives, feeds on questions because they keep it intelligently engaged.

A question is a wake-up alarm to the brain.

The fight-or-flight response is kept at bay by asking small questions the kaizen way. These questions include: “What can I do five minutes daily to become more efficient?” “How can I avoid spending more?” These types of questions allow us to downplay our fears. They assist the brain in concentrating on problem-solving and initiating actions.

As simple as it appears, consistently asking the same question and patiently awaiting a response stimulates the brain's cortex. The cortex internalizes the question and works on it subconsciously until it finds an answer.

A question is neither frightening nor demanding; this is why it is enjoyable for the brain. When posing minor questions, your amygdala — the brain's center for adrenaline reactions — stays dormant while the cortex becomes alert and attentive.

By crafting your inquiries in the kaizen style, you minimize the likelihood of triggering the amygdala and evoking overwhelming fear.

chapters.title: Visualize until you make it
chapters.text:
The mind can assist you in developing new social, cognitive, and physical abilities merely by visualizing yourself performing them.

While often encouraged to venture into the unknown or 'fake it till you make it,' there is a more effortless method to train yourself in complex tasks, even those that don't naturally align with your abilities and talents. The approach is called mind sculpture, — and it utilizes insights from neuroscience, indicating that the brain optimally learns through gradual steps.

Imagination and futuristic thinking play a major part in helping you achieve your desired goals.

Mind sculpture involves imaginary activities where practitioners engage in an action in their mind's eye, not only seeing but also hearing, tasting, smelling, and touching. For example, those who want to run a marathon imagine how their body moves and their emotions during the last kilometers.

In fact, many prominent athletes, such as Michael Jordan and Jack Nicklaus, have implemented mind sculpture to refine their skills!

Your brain's chemical makeup alters within minutes when you engage in a mental task using all your senses. This approach allows you to confront a difficult task with mental preparation, helping to bypass counterproductive fear and the hazards of a trial-and-error strategy.

The kaizen approach suits anyone struggling with a seemingly unattainable goal.

chapters.title: Carrying out small actions encourages success
chapters.text:
You can avoid obstacles that once defeated you by taking little steps that may seem easy or inconsequential. You also cultivate an appetite for continuous success.

Making changes by taking small steps is easier than waiting for a magic wand.

Using kaizen, your first action to achieve the desired outcome might be small, but its regularity eventually yields favorable results. Imagine you want to achieve a “stop overspending” goal.

A small step is always to have a budget before you go shopping and leave the house with just the right amount of money or maybe just a little extra for eventualities. Another example is an exercise goal. A small but valuable action would be to jog on the spot for a few minutes every morning. Both examples require minimal effort, which makes it easy to be consistent.

To learn a new language, you can memorize one new word daily or practice repeating the same unique word once or twice a day, adding something different each week.

If you have struggled to make a significant change over the years, practicing small steps aligning with your goals can help you achieve your desired changes.

At times, overly ambitious efforts to enact change can backfire. Minor actions are ideal as they demand minimal time or resources and deceive the brain into believing: “This alteration is so trivial; it's hardly significant.” Ultimately, they often result in swift change. So, there's no need to feel intimidated, upset, or overwhelmed.

Taking small steps requires optimism and patience. Never believe the steps are a waste because they are easy.

It's also important to embrace the delight of embarking on the initial step. This approach assists in determining the suitability of proceeding further.

When prepared for a new endeavor, your ongoing task becomes instinctive, effortless, and enjoyable. Refrain from rushing into further steps; wait until your mind overcomes its reluctance and propels you forward.

chapters.title: Identify and remove minor hindrances on your path to success
chapters.text:
It is pretty frustrating to identify and remove obstacles, but more annoying is that they obstruct your path to positive change. However, by learning to recognize and address minor issues now, you can prevent the need for more drastic solutions later.

Everyone loves incentives, and giving little rewards to yourself for every problem solved is the best encouragement toward achieving success. These rewards create the internal drive necessary for enduring change.

The brain is motivated to act when there's a reward in view. Use this to your advantage when procrastinating.

According to behavioral psychologist B. F. Skinner, positive reinforcement via reward systems helps shape human behavior quickly.

But rewards must be given with caution. Japanese executives usually offer small bonuses because they discovered that big prizes remove intrinsic motivation and creativity. They want employees to be more internally driven than externally motivated.

You can apply this in your life and wherever you find yourself acting as a leader, even with your kids.

Think hard before deciding on the perfect reward. Your rewards should have these three qualities:

• Be appropriate
• Be relevant
• Be inexpensive

Additionally, kaizen underpins strong relationships by encouraging attention to the finer aspects of a partner's life.

Through the kaizen technique, you can regain the ability to derive pleasure in the moment and become fascinated with whatever you're doing and with those around you.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
It's often said that a journey of a thousand miles starts with a single step. That's the foundation of the kaizen approach. This technique is old; we must acknowledge its efficacy, even in the modern world.

Kaizen involves using tiny moments and taking small steps to inspire change. It represents a smooth and natural approach to achieving objectives and sustaining high performance.

As discussed in this summary, employing mind sculpture in the true spirit of kaizen is an effective method for reaching your goals. It can assist you in realizing the progress you aspire to at a gentle and manageable pace.

Mind sculpture is the central part of kaizen. It involves engaging your mind to bring about desired change, from developing new habits to creating innovative products.

Mind sculpture involves several steps, which include:

• Identifying a task that you find daunting or intimidating.
• Determining the amount of time you are prepared to dedicate to mind sculpture.
• Practicing repetitively in a serene, comfortable setting while seated or lying down with closed eyes.
• Visualizing yourself in a challenging scenario and observing through your perspective.
• Expanding your imagination to encompass all your senses.
• Imagining that you are performing the task without moving any part of your body.
• Repeating this a couple of times is useful, and you can try small steps when you eventually feel ready. Ensure the steps align with the mental pictures you've created.

Is it possible to convince you that by allowing a driver to merge into your lane, expressing gratitude to a store clerk, or offering a smile to someone in a corridor, you might impact their lives? Probably not — but if you don't embrace the idea that minor moments and gestures can influence others, what's the contrasting belief?

Try this

Pick a goal you want to achieve in the next couple of months and do the following:

• Perform the mind sculpture steps to prepare your mind.
• Decide on small steps you'll take daily or weekly toward your goal.
• Be consistent.

Read it till the end?
